testName: displays Taskify heading and five user cards on launch
summary: Checks that the Taskify welcome screen appears when the app first opens, showing all five team members with their names and roles.
objective: Verify the landing page renders the app heading and all five user cards with correct names and roles.

flow.description: Open the app
The Taskify heading is visible at the top of the screen
A prompt reads 'Select your user to get started'
Five user cards are displayed showing Sarah Chen, Alex Rivera, Jordan Kim, Morgan Lee, and Taylor Patel
flow.action: Verify each user name is visible

flow.description: Each card shows the user's role (e.g., product manager, engineer)
flow.action: Verify each user role is visible

testName: each user card shows an avatar with the first letter of the name
summary: Confirms that every user card displays an avatar circle containing the first letter of the user's name (e.g., 'S' for Sarah Chen).
objective: Verify each user card renders a letter-based avatar using the first character of the user's name.

flow.description: Open the app
Look at each user card
Each card shows a circular avatar with the user's initial (S, A, J, M, T)
flow.action: Verify the first letter of the name appears inside the card

testName: clicking a user navigates to the project list
summary: Verifies that clicking on a user card takes you to the main project list view, showing the logged-in user's name and a Switch User button.
objective: Validate that selecting a user transitions the app from the user selection screen to the project list view.

flow.description: Open the app
Click on the 'Alex Rivera' card
The screen changes to show the project list
Alex Rivera's name appears in the header
flow.action: Check for user name in header

flow.description: A 'Switch User' button is visible
flow.action: Verify Switch User button is present

testName: no password prompt is shown
summary: Ensures the app does not ask for a password—users simply click their name to log in.
objective: Confirm that no password input field exists on the user selection screen.

flow.description: Open the app
Look for any password fields on the page
No password fields are found

testName: Switch User returns to the user selection screen
summary: Confirms that after logging in, clicking the 'Switch User' button takes the user back to the original selection screen.
objective: Verify the Switch User button navigates back to the user selection landing page.

flow.description: Open the app and select Sarah Chen
flow.action: Navigate to home and click Sarah Chen

flow.description: The project list appears with a 'Switch User' button
flow.action: Verify Switch User button is visible

flow.description: Click the 'Switch User' button
The user selection screen reappears with the Taskify heading
The instruction text 'Select your user to get started' is shown again

testName: board renders exactly 4 Kanban columns with correct headings
summary: After opening a project, the board displays four columns labeled To Do, In Progress, In Review, and Done.
objective: Verify the Kanban board renders all four status columns with the correct headings.

flow.description: Log in as Alex Rivera
flow.action: Select Alex Rivera from the user selection screen

flow.description: Open the 'Website Redesign' project
The board shows four columns
Columns are labeled 'To Do', 'In Progress', 'In Review', and 'Done'

testName: each column contains its expected task cards
summary: Checks that tasks from the sample data appear in the correct columns—for example, 'Design new homepage layout' is in To Do.
objective: Verify seed data task cards are rendered and visible in their expected columns.

flow.description: Log in as Alex Rivera and open Website Redesign
'Design new homepage layout' appears in the To Do column
'Implement responsive navigation bar' appears in In Progress
'Refactor CSS to Tailwind' appears in In Review
'Set up CI/CD pipeline' appears in Done

testName: columns show a card count badge
summary: Each column header displays a small badge showing how many task cards are in that column.
objective: Verify that each Kanban column displays a numeric count badge reflecting the number of cards it contains.

flow.description: Log in as Alex Rivera and open Website Redesign
Look at each column header
Each column shows a count badge (showing '1' for each column in the sample data)
flow.action: Verify the count badge text is visible next to each heading

testName: clicking a card opens the task detail modal
summary: Clicking on a task card opens a pop-up window showing the full details of that task.
objective: Verify that clicking a task card triggers the task detail modal overlay with the task's information.

flow.description: Log in as Alex Rivera and open Website Redesign
Click on the 'Design new homepage layout' card
A modal window appears on screen
flow.action: Verify the modal overlay is visible

flow.description: The modal shows the task title 'Design new homepage layout'
Close the modal by clicking the 'x' button

testName: back button returns to the project list
summary: Clicking the '← Projects' button on the board takes you back to the list of all projects.
objective: Verify the back navigation button returns the user from the Kanban board to the project list view.

flow.description: Log in as Alex Rivera and open Website Redesign
Click the '← Projects' button
The project list appears showing all projects
'Website Redesign' and 'Mobile App MVP' are both listed

testName: move a card from "To Do" to "In Progress" using keyboard drag
summary: A task card can be picked up and moved from the To Do column to In Progress using keyboard shortcuts, simulating a drag-and-drop action.
objective: Verify that keyboard-based drag and drop moves a card from the To Do column to the In Progress column.

flow.description: Log in as Alex Rivera and open Website Redesign
Focus the 'Design new homepage layout' card in the To Do column
flow.action: Give keyboard focus to the card

flow.description: Press Space to pick up the card
Press the Right Arrow key to move it to the next column
Press Space again to drop the card
The card now appears in the In Progress column
flow.action: Verify card is in the In Progress droppable area

testName: move a card from "In Progress" to "In Review"
summary: A task card in the In Progress column can be moved forward to the In Review column using keyboard drag.
objective: Verify keyboard drag moves a card from In Progress to In Review.

flow.description: Log in as Alex Rivera and open Website Redesign
Focus the 'Implement responsive navigation bar' card in In Progress
flow.action: Give keyboard focus to the card

flow.description: Pick up the card with Space, move right with Arrow, and drop with Space
The card now appears in the In Review column
flow.action: Verify card is in the In Review droppable area

testName: move a card backward from "Done" to "In Review"
summary: A task card can also be moved backward—from the Done column back to In Review—confirming that drag works in both directions.
objective: Verify that cards can be dragged backward (right-to-left) between columns using the left arrow key.

flow.description: Log in as Alex Rivera and open Website Redesign
Focus the 'Set up CI/CD pipeline' card in the Done column
flow.action: Give keyboard focus to the card

flow.description: Pick up the card with Space, move left with Arrow, and drop with Space
The card now appears in the In Review column instead of Done
flow.action: Verify card is in the In Review droppable area

testName: card remains in the board after drag (no data loss)
summary: After moving a card between columns, all four task cards are still present on the board—nothing is accidentally lost.
objective: Verify that dragging a card does not remove or duplicate any task cards on the board.

flow.description: Log in as Alex Rivera and open Website Redesign
Move the 'Design new homepage layout' card to a different column
flow.action: Perform keyboard drag to move the card

flow.description: Check that all four original tasks are still visible on the board
flow.action: Verify all task titles are present

flow.description: 'Design new homepage layout', 'Implement responsive navigation bar', 'Refactor CSS to Tailwind', and 'Set up CI/CD pipeline' are all accounted for
flow.action: Confirm no cards were lost or duplicated

testName: cards assigned to the current user have a distinct background color
summary: Task cards assigned to the currently logged-in user appear in a different background color, making it easy to spot your own tasks at a glance.
objective: Verify that the logged-in user's assigned cards have a visually distinct background color compared to cards assigned to other users.

flow.description: Log in as Alex Rivera and open Website Redesign
Look at the card assigned to Alex ('Implement responsive navigation bar')
flow.action: Get the background color of Alex's card

flow.description: Look at a card assigned to someone else ('Design new homepage layout')
flow.action: Get the background color of another user's card

flow.description: The two background colors are different
flow.action: Compare the two colors and confirm they differ

testName: card highlight updates when switching users
summary: When a different user logs in, the highlighting shifts to that user's cards—so the highlight always reflects who is currently using the app.
objective: Verify that card highlighting dynamically updates based on the currently logged-in user.

flow.description: Log in as Jordan Kim and open Website Redesign
flow.action: Navigate to the board as Jordan

flow.description: Jordan's card ('Design new homepage layout') is highlighted
flow.action: Get the background color of Jordan's card

flow.description: Alex's card ('Implement responsive navigation bar') is not highlighted
flow.action: Get the background color of Alex's card

flow.description: The colors differ, confirming the highlight follows the logged-in user
flow.action: Compare the two colors and confirm they differ

testName: unassigned cards use the default (non-highlighted) style
summary: Cards that are not assigned to anyone do not receive the special highlight color—they use the standard default background.
objective: Verify that unassigned task cards are rendered with the default (non-highlighted) background color.

flow.description: Log in as Alex Rivera
flow.action: Select Alex Rivera from the user selection screen

flow.description: Open the 'Mobile App MVP' project
Find the unassigned card 'Create onboarding flow'
The card's background color is the default white, not the highlight blue
flow.action: Verify background color is not the highlight color (rgb(239, 246, 255))

testName: add a new comment to a task
summary: A user can type a comment in the comment box and click Send to add it to a task. The new comment immediately appears in the list.
objective: Verify that a user can submit a new comment on a task and see it displayed in the comments section.

flow.description: Log in as Alex Rivera and open Website Redesign
Click on the 'Design new homepage layout' card to open the detail modal
The Comments section is visible
flow.action: Wait for the comments area to load

flow.description: Type 'This is a test comment from Alex' into the comment box
Click the Send button
The new comment appears in the list with Alex Rivera's name
flow.action: Verify the comment text and author are visible

testName: edit own comment
summary: A user can edit a comment they previously wrote by clicking Edit, changing the text, and saving. The updated text replaces the original.
objective: Verify that a user can modify the text of their own existing comment through the inline edit flow.

flow.description: Log in as Alex Rivera and open Website Redesign
Open the 'Implement responsive navigation bar' task
Find Alex's existing comment ('I am about halfway done with this...')
Click the Edit button next to Alex's comment
Clear the text and type 'Updated comment by Alex'
Click Save
The comment now reads 'Updated comment by Alex'
flow.action: Verify the updated text is displayed

testName: cannot edit or delete comments made by other users
summary: When viewing comments left by other people, the Edit and Delete buttons are hidden—you can only manage your own comments.
objective: Verify that Edit and Delete actions are not available on comments authored by a different user.

flow.description: Log in as Alex Rivera and open Website Redesign
Open the 'Design new homepage layout' task
Find Sarah Chen's comment ('Let us prioritize the hero section...')
Look for Edit and Delete buttons near Sarah's comment
No Edit or Delete buttons are shown for Sarah's comment
flow.action: Verify both buttons have zero count

testName: own comment shows Edit and Delete buttons
summary: When viewing your own comments, Edit and Delete buttons are visible, giving you full control to modify or remove what you wrote.
objective: Verify that Edit and Delete action buttons are displayed on comments authored by the currently logged-in user.

flow.description: Log in as Alex Rivera and open Website Redesign
Open the 'Design new homepage layout' task
Find Alex's comment ('Should we consider A/B testing...')
An Edit button is visible next to the comment
A Delete button is visible next to the comment

testName: delete own comment removes it from the list
summary: A user can delete a comment they wrote by clicking Delete and confirming. The comment disappears from the list immediately.
objective: Verify that deleting an own comment removes it from the UI after confirmation.

flow.description: Log in as Alex Rivera and open Website Redesign
Open the 'Design new homepage layout' task
Add a temporary comment ('Temporary comment to delete')
flow.action: Type and submit a new comment

flow.description: The temporary comment appears in the list
Click Delete next to the temporary comment
A confirmation dialog appears—click OK
The comment is no longer in the list
flow.action: Verify the comment text has zero occurrences
